N=12,812.In columns 3 and 4, we examine the effect of racial bias on the probability of receiving a PPP loan from a bank. In both regressions, the interaction between Black and Bias is negative and statistically significant. The magnitude of the effect is very large. A one standard deviation increase in racial bias is associated with a 17.6–19.1 percentage point reduction in the probability that a Black-owned restaurant receives a bank PPP loan, depending on whether we use explicit or implicit bias as the measure. Given that Black-owned restaurants are 28.7 (22.1) percentage points less likely to get a bank PPP loan in a county with average explicit (implicit) bias, this implies that Black-owned restaurants are 46.3 (41.2) percentage points less likely to receive bank PPP funding in counties with explicit (implicit) bias one standard deviation above the mean. The interactions between racial bias and the other minority restaurant-owner dummies are also negative, but smaller in magnitude and generally not statistically significant. It makes sense that although bias towards Black people may be correlated with bias against other minority groups, its strongest effect is on the likelihood that Black-owned restaurants receive bank PPP loans.In columns 5 and 6, we present results for nonbank PPP loans. Here the coefficient on the interaction between Black and Bias is 7.1–11.4 percentage points and the opposite of the sign for bank PPP loans. It is statistically significant at 10% for implicit but not explicit bias. These results provide suggestive evidence that Black-owned restaurants are more likely to substitute from bank to nonbank PPP loans in counties with greater racial bias. Finally, in columns 7 and 8, the dependent variable is whether the firm receives an EIDL loan. As with nonbank PPP loans, we find a positive interaction between Black and Bias, suggesting more substitution to EIDL loans in more biased counties.One may be concerned that racial bias is correlated with other county-level characteristics in a way that may explain differences in borrowing and drive the results in Table 10. Given the small number (66) of counties, it may be difficult to disentangle the effect of bias from other county characteristics. We therefore postpone doing so until we examine a broader sample of firms across four states.
article.6. Analysis of other industries and stat: Since restaurants were hit especially hard by the pandemic, one may wonder whether our results apply to other industries. Because we cannot identify the population of potential borrowers in other industries, we restrict our analysis to firms receiving other forms of government support that had similar eligibility criteria as the PPP. Specifically, we examine whether there are racial disparities in the take-up of PPP loans among the broader sample of firms that received EIDL Advance grants, just as we did in Table 5 for restaurants. One advantage of this approach is that because we are no longer limited to restaurants, we can expand our sample to include firms in other states with voter registration data that include self-reported racial and ethnic identity and are available to researchers at a modest cost. These states are Georgia, Louisiana, and North Carolina.38To create this broader sample, we start by matching EIDL Advance recipients to Florida, Georgia, Louisiana, and North Carolina corporate records, which we access through OpenCorporates, an open database of corporations around the world. We limit the resulting sample to for-profit corporations and LLCs that were active as of February 15, 2020. We next match PPP borrowers to these corporate records and use this link to determine which EIDL Advance recipients also received PPP loans.Because neither EIDL Advance nor OpenCorporates data include industry classification, we match firms to the Dun & Bradstreet database and use the firm’s primary SIC code in Dun & Bradstreet. Firms with SIC code 9999, non-classifiable establishments, are excluded from the analysis. We estimate regressions with ZIP code cross two-digit SIC industry fixed effects to compare minority- and white-owned firms that are in the same industry and same ZIP code; this corresponds to the approach in our analysis of Florida restaurants.We match each firm’s officers, directors, and agents to voter registration data using the same algorithm we use for our restaurants sample. However, we make two adjustments to our method of classifying firms based on the racial and ethnic identity of their officers. First, while Florida corporate records always report both registered agents who are designated to receive correspondence from the state and officers/directors who are authorized to act on behalf of the firm, not all other states do the same. In particular some states, including Georgia and North Carolina, report the registered agents but not necessarily officers of LLCs. With some firms using professional registered agents like CPAs and lawyers, we do not want to classify firms based on the racial identity of such professional agents. We therefore exclude individuals who are listed as registered agents of five or more firms.39 Second, in contrast to Florida corporate records, other states in our sample are less likely to list officers in order of seniority. Thus, instead of using the racial identity of the first listed officer we can match to voter registration, we set minority indicators equal to one if any of the matched officers report their racial or ethnic identity.Table 11 reports the results, with PPP take-up in columns 1–2, bank PPP take-up in columns 3–4, and nonbank PPP take-up in columns 5–6. Regression specifications control for ZIP-SIC2 fixed effects, thus enabling us to compare PPP take-up by firms in the same industry and ZIP code. We include the log of firm age, indicators for corporations (versus LLCs), and indicators for the implied number of employees based on the size of the EIDL Advance grant, although because of space limitations we do not report the coefficients of these variables. We also control for the interaction of other minority indicators with racial bias. To make sure that the results on the interaction between minority dummies and racial bias are not driven by other county and ZIP characteristics, we control for the interaction between minority indicators and (a) the log of county population, (b) the share of county population that identifies as Black, (c) the share of county population that identifies as white, (d) the gap in high school graduation rates between Black and white county residents, (e) the gap in unemployment rates between Black and white county residents, (f) the gap in household income between Black and white households, (g) number of bank branches per capita, and (h) the share of bank deposits in branches of the top 4 banks: Bank of America, Citigroup, JPMorgan Chase, and Wells Fargo. The full set of coefficients is reported in Table IA2 in the Internet Appendix. Standard errors are adjusted for clustering by county.In column 1, the coefficient on Black is −0.125. This means that a Black-owned EIDL Advance recipient with the average values of all county characteristics and without any UCC borrowing relationships is 12.5 percentage points less likely to receive a PPP loan than a comparable white-owned firm. Firms with Hispanic officers are 7.7 percentage points less likely to receive PPP loans, while firms with Asian officers are 1.7 percentage points more likely to receive PPP loans. Although the interpretation of the coefficients on the minority indicators is slightly more nuanced in Table 11, these results are broadly similar to the results in Table 5 for the sample of restaurants participating in the EIDL Advance program.Table 11. In a county that is one standard deviation above the mean of implicit racial bias, Black-owned firms are a further 4.0 percentage points less likely to receive PPP loans. These findings are driven by disparities in the take-up of bank PPP loans, which we demonstrate in columns 3–4. Column 4 shows that in a county that is one standard deviation above the mean of implicit racial bias, Black-owned firms are a further 7.9 percentage points less likely to receive bank PPP loans. In columns 5–6, the dependent variable is whether the firm received a nonbank PPP loan. These columns show evidence of substitution to nonbanks, especially in counties with stronger racial bias. For example, in column 6 the interaction between Black and implicit bias is 0.039, which is highly significant statistically and economically.Regression specifications in Table 11 also include interactions of Black with bank and nonbank UCC loan dummies. The interaction with bank UCC is around 0.066–0.068 in PPP and bank PPP regressions in columns 1–2 and 3–4, and is zero in the nonbank PPP regressions in columns 5–6. These results indicate that the disparity in the take-up of PPP loans is significantly smaller for firms with pre-existing bank UCC borrowing relationships.40Overall, the results in Table 11 show that there are large racial disparities in the larger sample of EIDL Advance recipients across all industries and four states. Black-owned firms are significantly less likely to receive PPP loans, especially in more biased counties. Pre-existing bank borrowing relationships and substitution to nonbanks help mitigate but do not eliminate disparities in PPP borrowing.
article.7. Conclusion: We have documented racial disparities in the Paycheck Protection Program and examined their causes. About 60% of disparities for Black- and Hispanic-owned restaurants can be explained by a combination of location and restaurant characteristics. Pre-existing borrowing relationships do little to explain disparities on average. Black-owned restaurants are still 9.1 percentage points less likely to receive PPP funding than similar white-owned restaurants. For Hispanic-owned restaurants the difference is a more modest 5.6 percentage points, and for Asian-owned restaurants and female-owned restaurants there is no appreciable difference in PPP take-up.Disparities in PPP borrowing are driven by disparities in PPP borrowing from banks. Black-owned restaurants are 16.8 percentage points less likely than white-owned restaurants to borrow from banks, while Hispanic-owned restaurants are 5.8 percentage points less likely, and Asian-owned restaurants are 6.1 percentage points less likely. Nonbanks — largely fintechs — make up for a portion of these disparities by lending at greater rates to Black-owned restaurants (7.6 percentage points), and they make up the entire difference for Asian-owned restaurants. Hispanic-owned restaurants borrow from nonbanks at the same rate as white-owned restaurants.The disparity in bank PPP borrowing for Black-owned restaurants appears to be exacerbated by racial bias. Restaurants located in counties where more white people express implicit or explicit biases towards Black people are less likely to receive PPP loans from banks. They are also more likely to substitute to nonbank PPP loans and EIDL loans. We see this basic pattern across a wide range of industries in Florida, Georgia, Louisiana, and North Carolina. Racial bias may have affected banks’ administration of PPP loans, or it may have reflected a legacy of bias that deterred Black restaurant owners from applying for PPP loans from banks. Our methodology cannot tell apart these two explanations, but our findings suggest that online, less personalized applications can help mitigate bias.41 Both fintechs and the SBA used an online application process with relatively less personal interaction than the application process used by many banks.Table A.1. We take the maximum of the number of Black CPAs in the ZIP of the restaurant itself and in the ZIP of the firm.At the same time, our results point to an important limitation of fintech lenders in expanding access and reducing racial disparities in access to credit. Because fintechs have limited capacity to help firms fix any deficiencies in their applications, fintechs work best when minority-owned businesses have access to professional help. Having just one more Black CPA in a firm’s ZIP code is associated with a 4.3 percentage point higher take-up of nonbank PPP loans by Black-owned firms.Our findings also point to potential issues with the design of the PPP that may have inadvertently affected the access of minority-owned businesses to PPP loans. While our focus has been on understanding racial disparities after controlling for differences in restaurant attributes — including proximity to bank branches, age, size, and borrowing history — these differences led to substantially lower PPP take-up by minority-owned restaurants. Indeed, these differences explain 60% of the disparity in borrowing by Black- and Hispanic-owned restaurants. While it is possible that some of the attributes of minority-owned restaurants may have lowered their demand for PPP loans, it is also possible that there were impediments to getting PPP loans.
